chapter_summary:
This chapter addresses the central computational problem of the SLB2011 framework: given a target pressure P_target and temperature T, determine the Eulerian finite strain f such that the total pressure P_ref(f) + P_thermal(f,T) equals P_target. This is the self-consistent pressure problem, and it arises because the thermal pressure itself depends on f (through the Gruneisen parameter, Debye temperature, and molar volume), creating a nonlinear coupling between the cold and thermal contributions to total pressure. The problem cannot be solved in closed form and requires an iterative numerical approach.

The MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer implements a pressure-correction (fixed-point iteration) algorithm. Starting with an initial estimate of f obtained by inverting the cold BM3 equation of state at the target pressure (via BM3Finite), it constructs the full ThermoMineralParams object that computes both P_ref(f) and P_thermal(f,T). If the computed total pressure differs from P_target, the reference pressure used in the next BM3Finite call is adjusted by the residual: P_ref_new = P_ref_old + (P_target - P_computed). This simple additive correction drives the solution toward self-consistency because the thermal pressure varies slowly with f compared to the cold pressure. Convergence is declared when |P_target - P_computed| < 1e-5 GPa, which corresponds to a pressure accuracy of 10 Pa -- far better than experimental uncertainties.

The inner root-finding step (BM3Finite) uses the Regula Falsi (false position) method to invert the nonlinear BM3 equation P(f) = 3K0(1+2f)^(5/2)f[1+1.5(K0'-4)f]. The search interval is bracketed between f_min = 0.0001 and a dynamically computed upper bound f_max = max(0.02, 3*P_est/(3*K0) + 0.01), ensuring robust convergence for both low-pressure and high-compression scenarios. Edge cases are handled explicitly: near-zero pressures (P < 0.0001 GPa) return a minimal strain of 0.0001 to avoid numerical difficulties, while the dynamic upper bound prevents bracket failure at very high pressures.

The algorithm typically converges in 3-10 outer iterations and 10-20 inner Regula Falsi iterations per outer step, making it computationally inexpensive. The maximum iteration count is set to 500 as a safety limit, and if convergence is not achieved, the best-effort result is returned with a convergence flag set to false. This architecture allows calling code to detect and handle rare non-convergence cases gracefully. The ThermoMineralParams result object carries not only all thermodynamic and elastic properties but also convergence metadata (IsConverged, Iterations, PressureResidual).
prerequisite_knowledge: Birch-Murnaghan equation of state and finite strain theory (Chapter 1)
Debye model and thermal pressure P_thermal = (gamma/V) * Delta_E (Chapter 2)
Gruneisen parameter gamma(f) and its strain dependence (Chapter 3)
Numerical root-finding methods: bisection, Regula Falsi, secant method
The complete SLB2011 parameterization of mineral properties (Chapter 5)

key_equations.equation: P_{\text{total}}(f, T) = P_{\text{ref}}(f) + P_{\text{thermal}}(f, T) = P_{\text{target}}
key_equations.name: Self-consistent pressure equation

variables.P_{\\text{ref}}: Cold (reference) pressure from BM3 EOS, P_ref = 3K0(1+2f)^{5/2}f[1+1.5(K0'-4)f] (GPa)

key_equations.interpretation: The fundamental equation that the MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer solves. Because P_thermal depends on f through gamma(f), V(f), and theta(f), the equation is implicitly nonlinear in f and must be solved iteratively.

key_equations.name: Mie-Gruneisen thermal pressure

variables.\\Delta E: Thermal energy difference from reference temperature (J/mol)
variables.n: Number of atoms per formula unit

key_equations.interpretation: The thermal pressure arises from the volume dependence of lattice vibrational energy. It is proportional to the Gruneisen parameter and inversely proportional to volume. At mantle temperatures (1500-3000 K), the thermal pressure contribution is typically 5-15 GPa.

key_equations.name: Pressure correction iteration

variables.P_{\\text{ref}}^{(i)}: Reference pressure used in the i-th iteration (GPa)
variables.P_{\\text{total}}^{(i)}: Total computed pressure in the i-th iteration (GPa)

key_equations.interpretation: The additive pressure correction scheme. If the computed total pressure is too low, the reference pressure (fed to BM3Finite) is increased, producing a larger f and hence a closer match. Convergence is rapid because P_thermal varies slowly with f compared to P_ref.

key_equations.name: Inner root-finding step
key_equations.interpretation: At each outer iteration, the cold BM3 equation is inverted numerically to find the strain that produces the adjusted reference pressure. This uses Regula Falsi root-finding on P(f) - P_ref = 0.

key_equations.name: Regula Falsi (false position) formula
key_equations.interpretation: A bracketed root-finding method that interpolates linearly between the function values at the bracket endpoints. More efficient than bisection for smooth, monotonic functions like the BM3 EOS. The bracket is updated to maintain sign change, guaranteeing convergence.

physical_concepts.definition: The problem of finding the finite strain f at which the sum of cold (BM3) pressure and thermal (Mie-Gruneisen) pressure equals a specified target pressure at a given temperature.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: At any point in Earth's mantle, the total pressure has two sources: the elastic compression of the lattice (cold pressure) and the thermal vibrations of atoms pushing outward (thermal pressure). These are coupled because the thermal pressure depends on volume (and hence strain), which in turn depends on total pressure. You cannot simply compute them independently -- you must find a self-consistent solution where everything is compatible.
common_misconceptions: Assuming thermal pressure is independent of strain -- in reality, gamma, V, and theta all depend on f, creating the coupling that necessitates iteration.
Thinking the cold BM3 pressure alone gives the total pressure -- thermal pressure can be 5-15 GPa at mantle temperatures, a significant fraction of the total.
Believing only one iteration is needed -- while the correction converges quickly, ignoring the feedback loop leads to errors of several GPa.

physical_concepts.definition: An equation of state that expresses the thermal pressure as P_th = (gamma/V) * (E(T) - E(T_ref)), where gamma is the Gruneisen parameter and E is the thermal (Debye) internal energy.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: The Mie-Gruneisen EOS separates pressure into a volume-dependent cold part and a temperature-dependent thermal part. The Gruneisen parameter gamma acts as the coupling constant between thermal energy and pressure. This decomposition is powerful because it allows the isothermal BM3 EOS (well-constrained by room-temperature experiments) to be extended to arbitrary temperatures using the Debye model for thermal energy.
common_misconceptions: Confusing the Mie-Gruneisen EOS with the BM EOS -- BM handles only the cold pressure; Mie-Gruneisen adds the thermal contribution on top.
Assuming the Mie-Gruneisen thermal pressure is always small -- at the base of the mantle (T ~ 3000-4000 K), it can reach 10-15 GPa.
Treating gamma as constant -- gamma depends on volume (strain), which is why the self-consistent iteration is necessary.

physical_concepts.definition: An iterative scheme where the reference pressure is adjusted by the residual (P_target - P_computed) at each step, converging to the solution where computed and target pressures match.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: Imagine you are trying to hit a target pressure. You start by asking the BM3 EOS for the strain at the target pressure, ignoring thermal effects. The resulting total pressure (cold + thermal) overshoots because thermal pressure adds to the cold pressure. So you reduce your reference pressure by the overshoot and try again. Each correction brings you closer because the thermal pressure changes only weakly when you adjust f by a small amount.
common_misconceptions: Expecting the iteration to diverge -- it converges because dP_thermal/df is much smaller than dP_ref/df, making the iteration contractive.
Thinking Newton's method would be needed -- the simple additive correction is effective because of the weak coupling.
Assuming convergence always requires many iterations -- typically 3-10 iterations suffice for 1e-5 GPa accuracy.

physical_concepts.definition: The iteration terminates when |P_target - P_computed| < 1e-5 GPa (10 Pa), ensuring the pressure is matched to high precision.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: A tolerance of 1e-5 GPa (0.01 MPa) is far smaller than any experimental pressure uncertainty (typically 0.1-1 GPa). This tight tolerance ensures that numerical errors in the self-consistent solver do not contaminate downstream calculations of density, elastic moduli, and seismic velocities. At the same time, it is easily achieved in a few iterations, so there is no computational penalty.
common_misconceptions: Thinking tighter tolerance means more physical accuracy -- the numerical precision far exceeds the accuracy of the underlying physical model and parameters.
Believing the convergence criterion should match experimental uncertainty -- numerical precision and physical accuracy are different concepts.

physical_concepts.concept: Edge case handling for near-zero and very high pressures
physical_concepts.definition: Special treatment for extreme pressure regimes: near-zero pressures return a minimal finite strain (f = 0.0001), and very high pressures use a dynamically expanded search bracket.
physical_concepts.physical_intuition: At near-zero pressure, the finite strain is essentially zero, and the BM3 root-finding can become ill-conditioned because the function is nearly flat. Returning a small default strain avoids numerical problems without introducing significant error (the resulting pressure error is < 0.1 GPa). At very high pressures, the initial bracket [0.0001, 0.02] might not contain the root, so the upper bound is scaled as f_max = max(0.02, 3*P/(3K0) + 0.01) using a linear pressure-strain estimate.
common_misconceptions: Assuming the BM3 can be inverted analytically for all pressures -- the nonlinear equation requires numerical root-finding.
Ignoring bracket failure at high pressures -- if the upper bracket is too small, the root-finder will converge to the wrong value or fail.

key_references.author: Stixrude, L. and Lithgow-Bertelloni, C.
key_references.title: Thermodynamics of mantle minerals - I. Physical properties
key_references.why_essential: Derives the complete self-consistent Mie-Gruneisen-Debye + finite strain framework that the optimizer implements. Equations for thermal pressure, strain-dependent Gruneisen parameter, and elastic moduli all originate here.

key_references.author: Stixrude, L. and Lithgow-Bertelloni, C.
key_references.title: Thermodynamics of mantle minerals - II. Phase equilibria
key_references.why_essential: Provides the mineral parameter database (SLB2011) and demonstrates the self-consistent pressure solver applied to phase equilibrium calculations across the full mantle pressure-temperature range.

key_references.author: Anderson, O.L.
key_references.title: Equations of State of Solids for Geophysics and Ceramic Science
key_references.why_essential: Comprehensive treatment of the Mie-Gruneisen equation of state, including its derivation from the Gruneisen assumption that thermal pressure is proportional to thermal energy divided by volume.

key_references.author: Poirier, J.-P.
key_references.title: Introduction to the Physics of the Earth's Interior (2nd ed.)
key_references.why_essential: Provides clear pedagogical derivation of the Mie-Gruneisen thermal pressure model and its coupling with the Birch-Murnaghan cold EOS.

key_references.author: Jackson, I. and Rigden, S.M.
key_references.title: Analysis of P-V-T data: constraints on the thermoelastic properties of high-pressure minerals
key_references.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors, 96, 85-112
key_references.why_essential: Discusses practical aspects of fitting Mie-Gruneisen-Debye EOS to experimental P-V-T data, including convergence of the self-consistent pressure iteration and parameter trade-offs.

worked_examples.problem: Compute the self-consistent finite strain and total pressure for forsterite (Mg2SiO4) at P_target = 14 GPa and T = 1600 K. Use parameters: V0 = 43.6 cm3/mol, K0 = 128 GPa, K0' = 4.2, theta0 = 809 K, gamma0 = 0.99, q0 = 2.1, n = 7.
worked_examples.solution_approach: Iteration 0: Start with P_ref = 14 GPa. BM3Finite(14) yields f ~ 0.0335. Construct ThermoMineralParams(0.0335, 1600, mineral). Compute P_thermal: gamma(f) ~ 0.97 (slightly reduced from gamma0 by strain), V(f) ~ 40.7 cm3/mol, Delta_E ~ 110 kJ/mol for n=7 atoms at 1600 K vs 300 K reference. P_thermal = (0.97/40.7)*110/1000 ~ 2.6 GPa. Total P = P_ref(0.0335) + P_thermal = 14 + 2.6 = 16.6 GPa. Residual = 14 - 16.6 = -2.6 GPa. Iteration 1: P_ref_new = 14 + (-2.6) = 11.4 GPa. BM3Finite(11.4) gives f ~ 0.0278. Recompute: P_thermal ~ 2.5 GPa. Total P = 11.4 + 2.5 = 13.9 GPa. Residual = 14 - 13.9 = 0.1 GPa. Iteration 2: P_ref = 11.4 + 0.1 = 11.5 GPa. Total P ~ 14.0 GPa. Converged within 1e-5 GPa after ~5 iterations.
worked_examples.interpretation: At 14 GPa and 1600 K (approximately the 410 km discontinuity), forsterite has a finite strain of about 0.028. The thermal pressure of ~2.5 GPa means the reference pressure used for BM3 inversion is only 11.5 GPa -- the thermal contribution accounts for about 18% of the total pressure. Without the self-consistent iteration, the strain and all derived properties would be significantly overestimated.

worked_examples.problem: Demonstrate the convergence behavior of the pressure correction algorithm for MgO periclase at P = 100 GPa, T = 2500 K. Use K0 = 161 GPa, K0' = 3.8, gamma0 = 1.52, theta0 = 767 K, n = 2.
worked_examples.solution_approach: This is a high-pressure case where P_thermal is large. Iteration 0: P_ref = 100 GPa, f = BM3Finite(100) ~ 0.098. P_thermal ~ (1.2/28)*65/1000 ~ 2.8 GPa. Total P = 102.8. Residual = -2.8 GPa. Iteration 1: P_ref = 97.2, f ~ 0.096, P_thermal ~ 2.7. Total = 99.9. Residual = 0.1. Iteration 2: P_ref = 97.3, Total ~ 100.0. Converged in ~4 iterations. Even at 100 GPa, the thermal pressure (~3 GPa) is a small fraction of total, so convergence is fast.
worked_examples.interpretation: Even at extreme lower-mantle conditions (100 GPa, 2500 K), the algorithm converges in only 4 iterations. The thermal pressure is only about 3% of the total at this high compression, because volume decreases faster than thermal energy increases. This confirms the algorithm's robustness across the full mantle pressure range.

worked_examples.problem: Show what happens at near-zero pressure (P = 0.0001 GPa, T = 300 K) for forsterite, demonstrating the edge-case handling.
worked_examples.solution_approach: When P_target < 0.0001 GPa, BM3Finite returns f = 0.0001 directly without root-finding. At T = 300 K (the reference temperature), P_thermal = 0 because Delta_E = E_Debye(300) - E_Debye(300) = 0. So the total pressure is simply P_ref(0.0001) = 3*128*(1.0002)^2.5*0.0001*[1+0.3*0.0001] ~ 0.0384 GPa. The residual is |0.0001 - 0.0384| = 0.038 GPa, which exceeds 1e-5. The algorithm then iterates, adjusting P_ref downward. After a few iterations it converges to a very small f corresponding to nearly ambient conditions.
worked_examples.interpretation: The near-zero pressure edge case is handled by clamping f to a minimum of 0.0001. This avoids numerical issues in the root-finder while introducing negligible error (the volume differs from V0 by only 0.03%). At ambient conditions, all computed properties closely match the reference values.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Ch01 Thermodynamics of Solids at High Pressure
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: The BM3 EOS P(f) and its inverse BM3Finite(P) from Chapter 1 form the inner core of the optimizer. The cold pressure P_ref and bulk modulus K_T(f) are both computed using the finite strain machinery introduced there.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Ch02 Debye Model and Thermal Properties
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: The thermal pressure P_thermal = (gamma/V)*Delta_E uses the Debye internal energy from Chapter 2. The DebyeFunctionCalculator provides E_Debye(T) at each iteration.

The strain-dependent Gruneisen parameter gamma(f) from Chapter 3 enters the thermal pressure calculation. The coupling between gamma and f is the fundamental reason the self-consistent iteration is necessary.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Ch04 Elastic Moduli and Seismic Velocities
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: Once the optimizer finds the self-consistent f, the ThermoMineralParams object computes K_T, K_S, G, Vp, and Vs using the methods from Chapter 4. The optimizer is the gateway to all elastic property calculations at arbitrary P-T.

The MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer is the computational engine of the SLB2011 framework described in Chapter 5. It takes the 11 SLB parameters and produces all thermodynamic and elastic properties at any P-T condition.

Rock property calculations (Chapter 10) require running the optimizer for each mineral endmember in the assemblage. The optimizer's efficiency directly impacts the feasibility of multi-mineral, multi-P-T calculations.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Ch12 Gibbs Free Energy and Phase Stability
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: Phase equilibrium calculations require computing Gibbs free energy G = F + PV at many P-T points. Each evaluation invokes the MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer, making its convergence speed critical for phase diagram construction.

connections_to_other_chapters.related_chapter: Ch28 Nonlinear Least Squares and Parameter Fitting
connections_to_other_chapters.explanation: Parameter fitting requires evaluating the forward model (optimizer) thousands of times in the inner loop of Levenberg-Marquardt or MCMC. The optimizer's reliability and speed are essential for practical fitting workflows.

computational_methods:
The MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer implements a two-level iterative scheme. The outer loop is a fixed-point (pressure correction) iteration, and the inner loop is a Regula Falsi root-finding for the BM3 inverse problem.

Outer loop (pressure correction): Starting with P_ref = P_target, the algorithm computes f = BM3Finite(P_ref), constructs a full ThermoMineralParams(f, T, mineral) to obtain P_total = P_ref(f) + P_thermal(f,T), and checks |P_target - P_total| < 1e-5 GPa. If not converged, it updates P_ref += (P_target - P_total) and repeats. This additive correction is a contraction mapping because dP_thermal/dP_ref is small (typically 0.02-0.05), ensuring rapid convergence. The maximum outer iteration count is 500.

Inner loop (BM3Finite): The Regula Falsi method is used to solve GetPressure(f) - P_ref = 0 within the bracket [0.0001, f_max]. The dynamic upper bound f_max = max(0.02, 3*P/(3*K0) + 0.01) uses a linear estimate P ~ 3*K0*f to set an appropriate search range. Convergence threshold is 1e-8 (on the pressure residual), with a maximum of 1000 iterations. The Regula Falsi method is preferred over bisection for its faster convergence on the smooth, monotonic BM3 function, and over the secant method for its guaranteed convergence within brackets.

Edge case handling: (1) Near-zero pressure (P < 0.0001 GPa): BM3Finite returns f = 0.0001 directly, bypassing root-finding. (2) Very high compression: the dynamic f_max ensures the bracket contains the root. (3) Non-convergence: after 500 outer iterations, the best-effort result is returned with IsConverged = false, Iterations = 500, and the final PressureResidual, allowing downstream code to detect and handle the failure.

Performance: A single optimizer call (one P-T point, one mineral) takes microseconds on modern hardware. The dominant cost is constructing ThermoMineralParams (which evaluates Debye functions) at each outer iteration. For typical mantle conditions, 3-10 outer iterations suffice, each with ~15 inner Regula Falsi iterations. The algorithm is called millions of times in applications like phase diagram scanning, lookup table generation, and MCMC inversion, so its reliability and speed are critical.

Alternative root-finding methods (Bisection, Secant) are also available via OptimizerFactory and can be selected through the OptimizerType enum, but Regula Falsi is the default due to its balance of speed and robustness.
open_questions: Could Newton-Raphson or Brent's method replace Regula Falsi for the inner BM3 inversion, and would the analytical Jacobian (available from BM3KT) provide meaningful speedup in practice?

For extreme P-T conditions (e.g., super-Earth interiors at >500 GPa), does the fixed-point pressure correction remain contractive, or would a more sophisticated nonlinear solver (e.g., Anderson acceleration) be needed?
How sensitive are the converged properties (Vp, Vs, density) to the convergence tolerance -- would relaxing from 1e-5 to 1e-3 GPa produce measurably different results for seismological applications?
Can the self-consistent pressure problem be reformulated as a direct minimization of Helmholtz free energy F(V,T) at fixed P, avoiding the iterative pressure matching entirely?
For minerals with Landau phase transitions or spin crossovers, do the additional pressure contributions from these effects change the convergence characteristics of the pressure correction algorithm?
exercise_suggestions: Implement the pressure correction algorithm by hand for forsterite at P = 24 GPa, T = 1800 K. Track P_ref, f, P_thermal, P_total, and residual at each iteration. How many iterations are needed for 1e-5 GPa convergence?

Compare the convergence rate of the pressure correction scheme at T = 300 K (where P_thermal ~ 0, so one iteration suffices) versus T = 3000 K (where P_thermal is large). Plot iterations-to-convergence as a function of temperature at fixed P = 50 GPa.

Modify the BM3Finite root-finding to use bisection instead of Regula Falsi. How many more inner iterations are needed for the same tolerance? Verify by running EOSOptimizerTests with OptimizerType.Bisection.
Demonstrate that the pressure correction is a contraction mapping by computing |dP_thermal/dP_ref| numerically. Show that this ratio is much less than 1 for typical mantle minerals.

Investigate the edge case at P = 0.0001 GPa, T = 2000 K. The thermal pressure at ambient strain can exceed the target pressure. Does the algorithm still converge? What is the physical interpretation of the resulting (slightly negative) effective cold pressure?

Compute the full set of thermoelastic properties (density, KS, GS, Vp, Vs) for MgO periclase along a 1600 K isotherm from 0 to 140 GPa in 5 GPa steps using MieGruneisenEOSOptimizer. Plot the results and compare with PREM.
Add a convergence counter to the optimizer and generate a map of iterations-to-convergence over the P-T space (0-140 GPa, 300-4000 K) for bridgmanite. Identify any regions where convergence is slow.

Derive analytically why the additive pressure correction converges: starting from P_total(P_ref) = P_ref + P_thermal(f(P_ref)), show that the derivative dP_total/dP_ref is close to 1, making the fixed-point iteration P_ref -> P_ref + (P_target - P_total) contractive.

figure_suggestions.description: Convergence of the pressure correction iteration showing P_ref, P_total, and residual versus iteration number
figure_suggestions.key_features: Show P_ref approaching ~11.5 GPa, P_total approaching P_target = 14 GPa, and |residual| dropping exponentially to below 1e-5 GPa. Use forsterite at 14 GPa, 1600 K as the example. Include a horizontal dashed line at P_target.

figure_suggestions.description: Decomposition of total pressure into cold (P_ref) and thermal (P_thermal) contributions along a geotherm
figure_suggestions.key_features: Stacked area plot showing P_ref dominating at high pressure while P_thermal remains a relatively constant 2-10 GPa band. Show for two minerals (forsterite and periclase) to highlight how gamma and volume affect the thermal pressure magnitude.

figure_suggestions.description: Number of iterations to convergence as a function of P and T
figure_suggestions.key_features: Color map showing iterations needed (typically 3-10). Highlight that convergence is fastest at low T (where P_thermal ~ 0) and remains fast even at extreme conditions. Note any regions where iterations exceed 10.

figure_suggestions.description: Comparison of root-finding methods (Regula Falsi, Bisection, Secant) for BM3 inversion
figure_suggestions.key_features: Show that Regula Falsi and Secant converge faster than Bisection. Mark the 1e-8 convergence threshold. Use a test case at P = 50 GPa with forsterite parameters.

figure_suggestions.description: Schematic flowchart of the two-level iterative algorithm
figure_suggestions.key_features: Box diagram showing: Input (P_target, T, mineral) -> Initialize P_ref = P_target -> Inner loop: BM3Finite(P_ref) -> f -> Outer: ThermoMineralParams(f,T) -> P_total -> Check |P_target - P_total| < 1e-5 -> if no, update P_ref -> loop; if yes, output results. Clearly label the two nested loops.

historical_context:
The Mie-Gruneisen equation of state has its roots in the early 20th century work of Gustav Mie (1903) on interatomic potentials and Eduard Gruneisen (1912) on the thermal properties of solids. Gruneisen's key insight was that the thermal pressure of a solid could be expressed as P_thermal = gamma * E_thermal / V, where gamma (now the Gruneisen parameter) captures how vibrational frequencies change with volume. This simple but powerful relation became the foundation for extending cold-compression equations of state to finite temperatures.

The combination of the Birch-Murnaghan cold EOS with the Mie-Gruneisen thermal model became standard practice in mineral physics during the 1970s-1980s, as diamond-anvil cell experiments began to provide P-V-T data at mantle conditions. Anderson (1995) provided a comprehensive treatment of the combined Mie-Gruneisen-Debye model in his influential textbook. The self-consistent pressure problem -- finding the volume (or strain) that satisfies both the cold and thermal pressure constraints simultaneously -- was recognized as a fundamental computational challenge.

Stixrude and Lithgow-Bertelloni (2005, 2011) placed the Mie-Gruneisen-Debye framework on a rigorous footing by deriving all thermodynamic quantities from a single Helmholtz free energy function. Their formulation made the self-consistent strain problem explicit: the strain-dependent Gruneisen parameter, Debye temperature, and volume create nonlinear coupling that requires iterative solution. The pressure correction algorithm implemented in the ThermoElasticCalculator is a direct realization of their approach, solving the coupled equations efficiently enough to enable applications ranging from single-mineral property computation to full mantle composition inversion.
